title: Crostata di marmellata
subtitle: Italian jam tart with lattice pastry
excerpt: A shallow Italian tart made with pasta frolla, a rich crumbly pastry flavoured with lemon zest and vanilla, spread with jam and finished with a woven lattice top. One of the most common Italian home bakes.
sourceNotes: Crostata is one of the oldest documented Italian sweet pastries. Bartolomeo Scappi, in his Opera dell'Arte del Cucinare (1570), describes pastry cases filled with fruit conserves. The domestic version using pasta frolla (shortcrust enriched with eggs and lemon zest) and a jam filling is documented throughout Italian domestic cookery from the eighteenth century and remains one of the most common bakes in Italian homes. Pellegrina Artusi, La Scienza in Cucina (1891), gives a standard recipe close to the one still used today.

content.text: : a rich, crumbly Italian shortcrust made with egg yolks, lemon zest, and a generous amount of butter. It is shorter than British shortcrust and fragile when warm, so it needs to be handled gently and chilled before lining the tin. The jam filling is spread directly on the unbaked base: no blind baking required. Any firm jam works; apricot or raspberry are the Italian standards.
Make the pasta frolla and chill

Rub the {{unsalted-butter}} into the {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{fine-salt}} until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Mix together the {{egg-yolks}}, {{lemon-zest}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} and add to the flour mixture. Bring together gently, do not overwork the dough or it will toughen. The dough will be slightly crumbly; press it together. Divide into two-thirds and one-third. Flatten each piece into a disc and chill for 30 minutes.
Line the tin and add the filling

Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Roll the larger disc out to about 3 mm thick on a lightly floured surface. Line a 24 cm tart tin (or a spring-form tin), pressing the pastry up the sides. Trim the overhang to 1 cm above the rim. Spread the {{apricot-jam}} evenly over the base, leaving a 1 cm border.

Roll out the remaining dough and cut into strips about 1.5 cm wide. Lay half the strips parallel across the tart, spaced evenly. Lay the remaining strips at a right angle, weaving over and under to form a lattice. Press the ends firmly onto the pastry rim and trim neatly. Brush the lattice and rim with a little beaten egg. Bake for 30-35 minutes until deep golden. Cool completely in the tin before slicing.
